content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

March 29, 2017

Richard E. Nosky, Jr.
City Attorney
Berliner Cohen LLP
Ten Almaden Blvd., 11th Floor
San Jose, CA 95113·2233

Re:    Your Request for Advice
       Our File No. A-17-068

Dear Mr. Nosky:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of
the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of
interest or Section 1090.

QUESTION

May Councilmember Lynnette Lee Eng participate in governmental decisions regarding a
kitchen upgrade at the Grant Park multi-use building and the Staff's decision to deny continued use
by a French immersion preschool of Park facilities, despite having a real property interest located
within 500 feet of the property?²

CONCLUSION

Yes. Councilmember Eng may participate in governmental decisions regarding a kitchen
upgrade at the Grant Park multi-use building and the termination of use by a French immersion
preschool's use of Park facilities.

_______________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² You have also asked if Councilmember Eng would have a conflict in decisions regarding the variety,
frequency, budget and size of various senior programs. However, we cannot offer advice without knowing the specific
details of the upcoming decisions regarding those programs.

File No. A-17-068
Page No. 2

FACTS

Councilmember Eng serves on the City of Los Altos ("City") City Council. She owns a
residence across the street from, and within 500 feet of, Grant Park, a public park located at 1575
Holt Avenue in Los Altos. The Park includes the Grant Community Center, a multi-purpose facility
with a stage, kitchen and four classrooms, and features a playground, basketball courts, and soccer
field.

The Grant Community Center includes four classrooms and a multi-purpose room. The
multi-purpose room has a capacity of 150 and is regularly scheduled for use by City-sponsored
programs, camps, classes and meetings, as well as non-City functions such as use by community
organizations and private rentals by individuals. It is a large, open room with a stage and small,
attached kitchen. The kitchen had previously been used for the Senior Meal Program, and, if
upgraded to a commercial kitchen, could be used for large special events like parties or weddings.

The City's Recreation & Community Services Department handles scheduling, and
prioritizes use of the facility for City and City-contracted programming. The City-Approved Fee
Schedule and City-Wide Free Use Non-Profit User List dictate what fees, if any, are paid for such
usage.

Adjacent to the kitchen is a space that has been converted into a senior lounge used by the
Wednesday Senior Program. During the time the seniors are not utilizing the lounge space, large
martial arts mats are stored there. Usage of the multi-purpose room is consistent throughout the
year, and includes Jazzercize, Jujitsu class, and the Wednesday Senior Program.

Other City and City-contracted programming uses include Lego camp, Commission
meetings, community meetings and Los Altos community group partner meetings and trainings
such as CERT or HAM-Radio groups. Non-City functions include youth sports groups and the
Community Church, and the supporters of these functions pay fees for the use of the facilities.
Local Girl Scout, Boy Scout and Cub Scout troops hold meetings in the activity rooms on an
ongoing basis and are part of the City-Wide Free Use Non- Profit User List.

The French Preschool began using the Grant Community Center in 2012. The preschool
operates between September and July, Monday-Friday, 8:30am-12:30pm, and occupies a
classroom, 1,900-square-foot outdoor fenced area, and has exclusive access to the multi-purpose
room restroom during its use time. The classroom is furnished with preschool-sized tables, chairs,
and other furniture. Enrollment is capped at a 15 student per day maximum.

According to City staff, a room that is set up for specific usage such as a senior lounge or
children's preschool, is much more difficult to utilize for other programming due to the usage-
specific furniture and room set-up. In these cases, the usage-specific furniture may restrict space
and make usage impossible.

Staff reviewed usage of the Grant Community Center and the programs that were currently
being offered in that location, and determined that an expansion of the senior programming was
desirable, as many of the Wednesday Senior Program participants communicated to staff that they
were interested in having an additional day of programming, specifically for social interaction. The

File No. A-17-068
Page No. 3

desire was to have a space that would accommodate discussion groups and card play. Staff
determined that the pre-school agreement needed to be terminated to maintain space for community
and City program usage.

Staff met with the preschool owners and stated that the City did not intend to approve any
requests for facility usage beyond the current school year. After the discussion, the owners came
back with a request for the same facility and the same time. Based on the priority factors, staff
maintained the decision to not approve the preschool's request for use of the Grant Community
Center for the 2017/2018 school year.

The City Council will be considering an upgrade to a full commercial kitchen in order to
continue and enhance the Senior Meal Program, as well as a discussion of and a possible vote on
the staff decision to terminate the preschool's use of the Grant Community Center.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest.
A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of the Act if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the
public official's interests, including an interest in real property. (Section 87103.)

A financial effect need not be likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable. (Regulation
18701(b).) In general, if the financial effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more
than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable. (Ibid.) If the financial result cannot be
expected absent extraordinary circumstances not subject to the public official's control, it is not
reasonably foreseeable. Under Regulation 18702.2(a), a governmental decision's effect on real
property is material if the decision:³

(10) Would change the character of the parcel of real property by
substantially altering traffic levels or intensity of use, including parking,
of property surrounding the official's real property parcel, the view,
privacy, noise levels, or air quality, including odors, or any other factors
that would affect the market value of the real property parcel in which
the official has a financial interest;

(11) Would consider any decision affecting real property value located
within 500 feet of the property line of the official's real property, other
than commercial property containing a business entity where the
materiality standards are analyzed under Regulation 18702.1.
Notwithstanding this prohibition, the Commission may provide written
advice allowing an official to participate under these circumstances if
the Commission determines that there are sufficient facts to indicate that

_________________________
³ Regulation 18702(b) cautions: "Notwithstanding Regulations 18702.1 through 18702.5, the financial effect of
a governmental decision is not material if it is nominal, inconsequential, or insignificant."

File No. A-17-068
Page No. 4

there will be no reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on the
official's property; or

(12) Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and
consideration under the circumstances, to believe that the governmental
decision was of such a nature that its reasonably foreseeable effect would
influence the market value of the official's property.

The decisions you describe involve an upgrade to a full commercial kitchen in a pre-existing
park facility and changes to the contract between the preschool and the City. Given the facts
presented, including the fact that the Park facilities are already used for a wide variety of programs,
there do not seem to be any significant changes to the character of the neighborhood that would
impact the Councilmember's property, none of the materiality standards set forth above are met.
Therefore, Councilmember Eng may participate in decisions regarding the upgrade of the kitchen
and the proposed change to the use agreement.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: Zachary W. Norton
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

ZWN:jgl

of property surrounding the official's real property parcel, the view,
